Lecture 8: Decomposition of Projections ​

Topics: §5.2–5.3 — Decomposition P=UV, VU=I Automatic, rank(P)=trace(P), Projection Splitting Date: Apr 6 – Apr 9, 2026

Introduction ​

In Lecture 7, we introduced projection operators through the sunlight-floor model and proved key properties from the defining equation P2=P. We discovered that:

  • Col(P)={v:Pv=v} (vectors on the floor)
  • Null(P)={v:Pv=0} (vectors pointing to the sun)
  • Col(P)∩Null(P)={0} (floor and sunlight are not parallel)

But we still have two fundamental questions unanswered:

::: question Two Natural Questions

  1. How to construct projections? Given a floor and a sunlight direction, how do we write down the matrix P?

  2. What makes projections special? We know from Chapter 1 that any matrix can be written as A=UV via cross-filling. What happens when we cross-fill a projection? :::

This lecture answers the second question. We will discover that decomposing projections reveals magical properties that do not hold for general matrices.

1. Decomposing Projections into Products ​

1.1 Review: Decomposing Any Matrix ​

Recall from Lecture 3 that any matrix A can be decomposed into a sum of rank-1 matrices:

A=R1+R2+⋯+Rr

where r=rank(A). We can collect these rank-1 pieces into a product form:

A=UV

where:

  • U is an m×r matrix whose columns are the "left parts" of each Ri
  • V is an r×n matrix whose rows are the "right parts" of each Ri

Example

Example 1.1: Decomposing a General Matrix

A=(2412)=(21)(12)=(21)(12)

Here U=(21) (one column) and V=(12) (one row).

We can verify: UV=(21)(12)=(2412)=A. ✓

But what about VU?

VU=(12)(21)=1⋅2+2⋅1=4≠I

For general matrices, VU is just some number or matrix — nothing special.

1.2 What Happens When We Cross-Fill a Projection? ​

Now let's try the same process with a projection matrix.

Example

Example 1.2: Decomposing a Projection

Consider the projection onto the x-axis:

P=(1000)

Verify it's a projection: P2=(1000)(1000)=(1000)=P. ✓

Cross-fill it:

P=(10)(10)

So U=(10) and V=(10).

Now compute VU:

VU=(10)(10)=1=I1

Remarkable! For this projection, VU=I.

Is this just a coincidence, or does it always happen?

Example

Example 1.3: Another Projection

Consider the projection onto the line y=x:

P=12(1111)

Verify: P2=12(1111)⋅12(1111)=14(2222)=12(1111)=P. ✓

Cross-fill it:

P=12(11)(11)

So U=12(11) and V=12(11) (we can scale to normalize).

Actually, let's just take U=(11) and V=12(11).

Then:

VU=12(11)(11)=12(1+1)=1=I1

Again, VU=I!

This pattern suggests a deep theorem.

2. Decomposition and the VU = I Property ​

2.1 Statement ​

Proposition

Theorem 2.1 (VU = I Automatic)

Let P be a projection (P2=P). Suppose P=UV where:

  • U is m×r with full column rank (rank r)
  • V is r×n with full row rank (rank r)

Then VU=Ir automatically.

Remark

Why Is This Remarkable?

For a general matrix A=UV, the product VU can be anything:

  • It might not even be square (if U and V have different numbers of columns/rows)
  • Even if square, it's typically not the identity

But for projections, P2=P forces VU=I automatically — no additional conditions needed!

2.2 Proof ​

The proof uses only the defining property P2=P and basic matrix algebra.

Given: P=UV and P2=P.

Step 1: Expand P2=P using P=UV:

UVUV=UV

Step 2: Apply left cancellation to U.

Since U has rank r (full column rank), its columns are linearly independent. From Lecture 4, we know linearly independent columns allow left cancellation:

UVUV=UV⟹VUV=V

Step 3: Apply right cancellation to V.

Since V has rank r (full row rank), its rows are linearly independent, which allows right cancellation:

VUV=V⟹VU=Ir

Done! ∎

3. Rank Equals Trace for Projections ​

The automatic property VU=I leads to a stunning consequence.

Proposition

Theorem 3.1 (Rank = Trace for Projections)

For any projection P (satisfying P2=P):

rank(P)=trace(P)

Proof:

Write P=UV where rank(P)=r. By Theorem 2.1, VU=Ir.

Using the cyclic property of trace:

trace(P)=trace(UV)=trace(VU)=trace(Ir)=r=rank(P)

Done! ∎

Remark

Why Doesn't This Work for General Matrices?

For a general matrix A=UV, we do NOT have VU=I.

Even though trace(A)=trace(UV)=trace(VU), the matrix VU is NOT the identity, so we cannot conclude that trace(A)=rank(A).

This is why rank = trace is special to projections.

3.1 Characterization of Rank-1 Projections ​

For rank-1 projections, we have a beautiful characterization.

Proposition

Corollary 3.2 (Rank-1 Projection Characterization)

For a square matrix A, consider the following three conditions:

  1. rank(A)=1
  2. trace(A)=1
  3. A2=A (i.e., A is a projection)

If any two of these conditions hold, then all three hold.

Proof:

We prove each of the three implications:

(1) + (2) ⟹ (3): Suppose rank(A)=1 and trace(A)=1.

Since rank is 1, we can write A=uvT for some vectors u,v.

The trace is:

trace(A)=trace(uvT)=vTu=1

Now compute A2:

A2=(uvT)(uvT)=u(vTu)vT=u⋅1⋅vT=uvT=A

So A is a projection. ✓

(1) + (3) ⟹ (2): Suppose rank(A)=1 and A2=A.

Write A=UV where U is n×1 and V is 1×n (since rank is 1).

By Theorem 2.1, VU=I1 (i.e., VU=1, a scalar).

Therefore:

trace(A)=trace(UV)=trace(VU)=trace(1)=1

✓

(2) + (3) ⟹ (1): Suppose trace(A)=1 and A2=A.

By Theorem 3.1, rank(A)=trace(A)=1. ✓

Done! ∎

Remark

Why Is This Useful?

This characterization tells us that rank-1 projections are exactly the matrices with rank 1 and trace 1.

We don't need to check A2=A separately — the projection property comes for free from rank and trace!

This will be crucial in Section 5 when we decompose projections into rank-1 pieces.

3.2 When Are Both U and V Square? ​

An interesting question: if UV=I, when are both U and V square matrices?

Proposition

Corollary 3.3 (UV = I Forces Square Matrices)

Suppose U is n×r and V is r×n with UV=In and r≤n.

Then n=r, and both U and V are square invertible matrices with VU=UV=I.

Proof:

Step 1: Observe that VU is a projection.

VU⋅VU=V(UV)U=VInU=VU

So VU is an r×r projection.

Step 2: Show that Ir−VU is also a projection.

(Ir−VU)2=Ir−2VU+(VU)2=Ir−2VU+VU=Ir−VU

Step 3: Compute the trace.

trace(Ir−VU)=trace(Ir)−trace(VU)=r−trace(VU)

But trace(VU)=trace(UV)=trace(In)=n.

Therefore:

trace(Ir−VU)=r−n

Step 4: Apply rank = trace.

Since Ir−VU is a projection, by Theorem 3.1:

rank(Ir−VU)=trace(Ir−VU)=r−n

Step 5: Conclude.

Since rank ≥0, we have r−n≥0, so r≥n.

Combined with the assumption r≤n, we get r=n.

Therefore trace(Ir−VU)=0, which means rank(Ir−VU)=0.

A matrix with rank 0 is the zero matrix, so Ir−VU=0.

Hence VU=Ir. ∎

Remark

Why Is This Surprising?

This says: You cannot have UV=I with U thin and V fat (or vice versa).

If the product is the identity, both matrices must be square and invertible, and the product commutes: UV=VU=I.

3.3 Full-Rank Square Matrix is Invertible ​

Corollary 3.3 establishes a key property of square matrices with one-sided inverses. We now use this result to prove the fundamental invertibility criterion.

Proposition

Proposition 3.4 (Full-Rank Square Matrix is Invertible)

An n×n matrix X is invertible if and only if rank(X)=n (full rank).

Proof:

Direction (⇒): If X is invertible, then rank(X)=n.

Suppose XY=I for some matrix Y. Note that I is a projection (I2=I) with rank(I)=n.

By cross-filling (Lecture 3), decompose X=R1+⋯+Rk where each Ri is rank-1 and k=rank(X). Then:

(R1+⋯+Rk)Y=I

This gives I=R1Y+⋯+RkY.

Since rank(RiY)≤rank(Ri)=1 for each i, we have:

∑i=1krank(RiY)≤∑i=1krank(Ri)=k

Since k≤n and rank(I)=n, we can apply Theorem 5.1 (part 3): if a projection P can be written as P=S1+⋯+Sm with ∑rank(Si)≤rank(P), then automatically ∑rank(Si)=rank(P).

Therefore ∑i=1krank(RiY)=rank(I)=n, which implies k≥n.

Since k=rank(X)≤n (for an n×n matrix), we conclude k=n.

Direction (⇐): If rank(X)=n, then X is invertible.

By cross-filling, X=UV where rank(X)=n.

Since X is n×n with rank n, the decomposition requires exactly n rank-one pieces, so U and V must both be n×n square matrices.

Construct a one-sided inverse for V:

Consider the augmented matrix (X∣In) and apply cross-filling using the same pivot selections as used to decompose X:

  • Each pivot is selected from the X-part (not from In)
  • Each pivot clears its row and column

Since all pivots are in X, the X-part decomposes exactly as before: X=UV. However, In is affected by the same row operations—each time a pivot row is selected, the corresponding row of In is "bombarded" and transformed.

Since rank(X)=n, we select n pivots covering all n rows. Therefore In is completely "bombarded" through all rows, transforming into some matrix F.

The cross-filling structure gives:

(X∣In)=(U∣F)V

Comparing both sides:

  • Left part: X=UV ✓
  • Right part: In=FV

Therefore FV=In. Since V is square, by Corollary 3.3 (if FV=I for square matrices, then VF=I), we have V invertible.

Construct a one-sided inverse for U:

Similarly, consider (XIn) and apply cross-filling using column selections (all pivots selected from X-part). Since rank(X)=n (equal to the number of columns), all n columns are covered, so In is completely bombarded through all columns, giving:

(XIn)=U(VG)

Thus In=UG. By Corollary 3.3, U is invertible.

Conclusion: X=UV with both U,V invertible, so X is invertible. □

Remark

Key Insight from Cross-Filling

This proof shows that when rank(X)=n (full rank), the cross-filling decomposition X=UV automatically produces invertible factors U and V.

The augmented matrix technique simultaneously reveals one-sided inverses:

  • (X∣I)=(U∣F)V gives FV=I, so F=V−1
  • (XI)=U(VG) gives UG=I, so G=U−1

This connects to the standard method of finding X−1 by row-reducing (X∣I)→(I∣X−1).

4. Compatible Families of Projections ​

Now we explore what happens when we have multiple projections that add up to the identity.

4.1 Definition ​

Definition

Compatible Family of Projections

A collection of projection matrices {P1,P2,…,Pk} (each satisfying Pi2=Pi) is called a compatible family if:

P1+P2+⋯+Pk=I

and

PiPj=0for all i≠j

Example

Example 4.1: Projections onto Coordinate Axes

In R3, consider:

P1=(100000000),P2=(000010000),P3=(000000001)

Check:

  • Each Pi2=Pi ✓
  • P1+P2+P3=I3 ✓
  • PiPj=0 for i≠j ✓

This is a compatible family.

4.2 Geometric Interpretation ​

Remark

What Does Compatibility Mean Geometrically?

If {P1,…,Pk} is a compatible family with P1+⋯+Pk=P:

  1. Unique decomposition: Every vector v satisfies:

    Pv=P1v+P2v+⋯+Pkv

    and this decomposition is unique (any element of Col(P) can be uniquely written as a sum of elements from Col(P1),…,Col(Pk)).

  2. PiPj=0 for i≠j means: the "floors" are mutually orthogonal in the sense that:

    Col(Pi)∩Col(Pj)={0}for i≠j

The space is decomposed into k subspaces that are "perpendicular" (in a generalized sense).

4.3 Key Properties of Compatible Families ​

Compatible families have beautiful properties related to linear independence and closure under subsums.

Proposition

Theorem 4.1 (Properties of Compatible Families)

Let {P1,…,Pk} be a compatible family of projections. Then:

  1. Vector decompositions are linearly independent: For any vector v, the nonzero vectors among {P1v,…,Pkv} are linearly independent.

  2. Subsums are projections: For any subset I⊆{1,…,k}, the sum ∑i∈IPi is also a projection.

Proof of (1):

Suppose c1P1v+⋯+ckPkv=0.

Left-multiply both sides by Pj for any j:

Pj(c1P1v+⋯+ckPkv)=0

Expanding:

c1PjP1v+⋯+cjPjPjv+⋯+ckPjPkv=0

Since PjPi=0 for i≠j and PjPj=Pj:

cjPjv=0

If Pjv≠0, then cj=0.

Therefore, all coefficients of nonzero vectors must be zero, proving linear independence. ∎

Proof of (2):

Let Q=∑i∈IPi. We need to show Q2=Q.

Q2=(∑i∈IPi)(∑j∈IPj)=∑i∈I∑j∈IPiPj

Since PiPj=0 for i≠j and PiPi=Pi:

Q2=∑i∈IPiPi=∑i∈IPi=Q

∎

Remark

The Direct Sum Interpretation

Property (1) is the vector-level statement of direct sum decomposition:

If {P1,…,Pk} is compatible with P1+⋯+Pk=P, then:

Pv=P1v+P2v+⋯+Pkv

is the unique way to write Pv as a sum of vectors from Col(P1),…,Col(Pk).

This is exactly the definition of direct sum:

Col(P)=Col(P1)⊕Col(P2)⊕⋯⊕Col(Pk)

5. Decomposition of Projections ​

Here comes the most surprising result of this lecture.

5.1 The Question ​

Suppose we have a projection P decomposed into a sum of matrices:

P=R1+R2+⋯+Rk

We don't know yet whether the Ri are projections. We only know:

∑i=1krank(Ri)≤rank(P)

Question: Can we conclude that each Ri is a projection and that they are mutually orthogonal (RiRj=0 for i≠j)?

Surprisingly, the answer is yes!

5.2 The Theorem ​

Proposition

Theorem 5.1 (Projection Decomposition)

Let P be a projection (P2=P). Suppose:

P=R1+R2+⋯+Rk

where each Ri is a matrix satisfying:

∑i=1krank(Ri)≤rank(P)

Then automatically:

  1. Each Ri is a projection (Ri2=Ri)
  2. RiRj=0 for all i≠j
  3. ∑i=1krank(Ri)=rank(P) (equality holds)

Remark

Why Is This Remarkable?

We don't assume the Ri are projections — the projection property emerges automatically from the rank condition!

This is the power of the rank = trace theorem combined with decomposition.

5.3 Proof Using the R₀ Trick ​

The key insight is to introduce a "complementary" matrix to make the sum equal to the identity.

Proof:

Let R0=I−P. Note that R0 is also a projection:

R02=(I−P)2=I−2P+P2=I−2P+P=I−P=R0

Now we have:

In=R0+R1+⋯+Rk

Step 1: Count total rank.

∑i=0krank(Ri)=rank(R0)+∑i=1krank(Ri)≤rank(R0)+rank(P)

Since R0=I−P is a projection, by Theorem 3.1:

rank(R0)=trace(R0)=trace(I−P)=n−trace(P)=n−rank(P)

Therefore:

∑i=0krank(Ri)≤(n−rank(P))+rank(P)=n

Step 2: Decompose each Ri.

For each i=0,1,…,k, decompose Ri=UiVi where Ui is n×ri and Vi is ri×n with ri=rank(Ri).

Collect all pieces:

U=(U0U1⋯Uk),V=(V0V1⋮Vk)

Then:

UV=∑i=0kUiVi=∑i=0kRi=In

The matrix U is n×(∑i=0kri) and V is (∑i=0kri)×n.

Step 3: Apply Corollary 3.3.

Since UV=In and ∑i=0kri≤n (from Step 1), by Corollary 3.3, both U and V must be n×n square matrices, and:

VU=In

This forces ∑i=0kri=n (equality must hold).

Step 4: Block structure.

Since VU=In, we have:

(V0V1⋮Vk)(U0U1⋯Uk)=(V0U0V0U1⋯V0UkV1U0V1U1⋯V1Uk⋮⋮⋱⋮VkU0VkU1⋯VkUk)=In

The only way this equals In is if the matrix is block diagonal:

ViUj=0for all i≠j,andViUi=Iri

Step 5: Prove each Ri is a projection.

For each i:

Ri2=(UiVi)(UiVi)=Ui(ViUi)Vi=UiIriVi=UiVi=Ri

So each Ri is a projection! ✓

Step 6: Prove orthogonality.

For i≠j:

RiRj=(UiVi)(UjVj)=Ui(ViUj)Vj=Ui⋅0⋅Vj=0

So RiRj=0 for all i≠j! ✓

Done! ∎

Remark

The Magic of Decomposing

This proof would be much harder without the cross-filling perspective!

The key insight is:

  1. Trace = rank tells us how many columns U must have
  2. VU = I forces a block diagonal structure
  3. The block diagonal structure forces orthogonality

All of this flows naturally from P2=P. No additional conditions needed!

6. Summary and Looking Ahead ​

In this lecture, we discovered the magical properties of decomposing projections:

TIP

Key Theorems

  1. VU = I Automatic (Theorem 2.1): If P=UV with P2=P and U,V full rank, then VU=I.

  2. Rank = Trace (Theorem 3.1): For any projection P, rank(P)=trace(P).

  3. Rank-1 Characterization (Corollary 3.2): For square matrix A, any two of {rank=1, trace=1, A2=A} imply the third.

  4. UV = I Forces Square (Corollary 3.3): If UV=I with U thin and V fat, then both must be square and VU=I.

  5. Projection Decomposition (Theorem 5.1): If P=R1+⋯+Rk with ∑rank(Ri)≤rank(P), then each Ri is a projection and RiRj=0.

These results show that projections have extremely rigid structure. The single equation P2=P imposes so many constraints that:

  • Decomposition automatically produces VU=I
  • Rank equals trace
  • Sum decompositions automatically produce mutually orthogonal projections

::: question What's Next?

We now understand the internal structure of projections (via cross-filling). But we still haven't answered our first question from the introduction:

How do we construct a projection from scratch?

Given:

  • A "floor" (a subspace W⊆Rn)
  • A "sunlight direction" (another subspace S⊆Rn)

How do we write down the matrix P that projects onto W along S?

This is the topic of Lecture 9: Constructing Projections. :::

Exercises ​

Problem

Exercise 8.1: Verify VU=I for the projection P=15(1224).

Exercise 8.2: Find two projections P1,P2 in R2 such that P1+P2=I and verify that P1P2=0.

Exercise 8.3: Show that if P is a projection, then trace(P)=dim⁡(Col(P)).

Exercise 8.4: Let P=(100010000) and Q=(000000001). Verify that {P,Q} is a compatible family.

Exercise 8.5: (Challenge) Suppose P1,P2,P3 are projections with P1+P2+P3=P where P2=P. Prove that P1,P2,P3 are linearly independent matrices (as elements of the vector space of n×n matrices).

Exercise 8.6: (Challenge) Let A be a full-rank square matrix (i.e., A is n×n and rank(A)=n). Suppose A=R1+R2+⋯+Rn is the decomposition of A into a sum of rank-1 matrices (via cross-filling).

Prove that A−1Ri is a projection for each i=1,…,n.

Furthermore, show that {A−1R1,…,A−1Rn} forms a compatible family of projections.

Hint: Multiply both sides of A=R1+⋯+Rn by A−1. What projection do you get on the left side? Now apply Theorem 5.1.
